encounters.reason: Annual wellness, diabetes and hypertension follow-up
encounters.header: 2021-09-14 — Office Visit — Annual wellness, diabetes and hypertension follow-up
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker was seen by Dr. Monica Reyes, MD, at Desert Family Medicine in Phoenix, Arizona for her annual primary care visit and chronic disease follow-up. Full intake vitals were BP 148/88 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 98.2 F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 198 lb, height 64 in, BMI 34.0 kg/m2. PHQ-2 score was 1/6, negative for major depression. Diabetic foot exam showed intact skin, dorsalis pedis pulses 2+ bilaterally, and mildly reduced monofilament sensation at the plantar forefeet without ulcers. Fasting labs ordered and reviewed included hemoglobin A1c 7.8%, serum glucose 156 mg/dL, creatinine 1.18 mg/dL with eGFR about 53 mL/min/1.73 m2, urine albumin-creatinine ratio 68 mg/g, total cholesterol 194 mg/dL, LDL 112 mg/dL, HDL 46 mg/dL, triglycerides 181 mg/dL, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, AST 19 U/L, and ALT 22 U/L. Type 2 diabetes mellitus remained above goal but stable on metformin 1000 mg twice daily, hypertension was still suboptimally controlled on amlodipine 10 mg daily and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, and hyperlipidemia persisted. Atorvastatin was increased from 20 mg to 40 mg nightly, and lisinopril 10 mg daily was retried for renal protection but documented again as previously causing cough, with plan to stop if cough recurred. Influenza vaccine was administered and documentation noted prior COVID-19 primary series completed earlier in 2021. A primary care progress note, preventive care update, vaccine record, and patient instructions were generated, with follow-up planned in 4 months for BP, diabetes labs, and medication tolerance.

encounters.reason: HFrEF and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation follow-up
encounters.header: 2022-06-08 — Office Visit — HFrEF and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation follow-up
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker saw Dr. Aaron Patel, MD, at Valley Heart Institute in Phoenix, Arizona for cardiology follow-up after workup earlier in 2022 had established heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Vitals were BP 132/80 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, irregularly irregular at intake then regular on repeat ECG, RR 18/min, Temp 97.9 F, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 201 lb, height 64 in, BMI 34.5 kg/m2. She reported exertional dyspnea after one flight of stairs and intermittent palpitations but no syncope or chest pain. Review of testing included transthoracic echocardiogram showing LVEF 35%, mild left ventricular dilation, global hypokinesis, grade II diastolic dysfunction, and mild left atrial enlargement; same-day ECG showed sinus rhythm at 72 bpm with premature atrial complexes, while prior ambulatory monitor had captured brief atrial fibrillation runs up to 140 bpm. Labs drawn for medication safety showed BNP 412 pg/mL, creatinine 1.31 mg/dL, eGFR about 46, potassium 4.5 mmol/L, magnesium 1.9 mg/dL, and TSH 1.7 uIU/mL. HFrEF was assessed as NYHA class II to III and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation carried a CHA2DS2-VASc score supporting anticoagulation. Carvedilol 6.25 mg twice daily and furosemide 20 mg each morning were continued, losartan was stopped in favor of starting sacubitril/valsartan 24/26 mg twice daily after appropriate washout due to ACE-intolerance history, and apixaban 5 mg twice daily was started for stroke prevention. A cardiology progress note, ECG interpretation, echocardiography review, medication list update, and sodium/fluid restriction instructions were completed, with PA-C Lisa Nguyen scheduled to reassess tolerance and titration in 3 to 4 weeks.

encounters.reason: Diabetes, CKD, and neuropathy worsening during caregiving stress
encounters.header: 2023-11-16 — Office Visit — Diabetes, CKD, and neuropathy worsening during caregiving stress
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker returned to Desert Family Medicine in Phoenix, Arizona and was evaluated by Samuel Hart, FNP-C, for worsening diabetes control, chronic kidney disease monitoring, and burning foot pain while under marked caregiving stress. Intake vitals were BP 154/92 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 98.4 F, SpO2 98%, weight 192 lb, height 64 in, BMI 33.0 kg/m2. PHQ-2 score was 2/6 with grief and stress discussed, but she denied self-harm thoughts. She described missed meals, inconsistent medication timing, nocturnal burning pain in both feet, and increased numbness of the toes. Foot exam showed diminished monofilament sensation at 6/10 points bilaterally, reduced vibration at the great toes, no ulcers, and callus formation at the metatarsal heads. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin A1c 9.4%, fasting glucose 214 mg/dL, creatinine 1.56 mg/dL, eGFR about 38, BUN 29 mg/dL, potassium 4.7 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, urine albumin-creatinine ratio 342 mg/g, LDL 92 mg/dL, and hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL. Type 2 diabetes was assessed as poorly controlled with diabetic peripheral neuropathy and diabetic kidney disease; CKD was documented as stage 3b by trend; hypertension remained above goal. Because metformin was causing GI upset and renal reserve had worsened, metformin was stopped. Insulin glargine was increased from 18 units to 24 units nightly with titration instructions to increase by 2 units every 3 nights until fasting glucose was under 140 mg/dL, empagliflozin 10 mg daily was continued, gabapentin 300 mg nightly was started for neuropathic pain, and atorvastatin 40 mg nightly was renewed. Progress note, updated problem list, diabetic foot exam documentation, titration instructions, and referral paperwork for ongoing cardiology and future nephrology review were completed; repeat labs were planned in about 3 months, but follow-up was expected to be disrupted by her upcoming move.

encounters.site_name: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic
encounters.reason: Acute decompensated heart failure after relocation and medication lapse
encounters.location: University of New Mexico Hospital, Albuquerque, New Mexico
encounters.header: 2024-04-22 — Inpatient Hospitalization — Acute decompensated heart failure after relocation and medication lapse
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker presented to the University of New Mexico Hospital in Albuquerque, New Mexico and was admitted under Dr. Priya Nanduri, MD, for acute decompensated heart failure after several weeks of disrupted medication access following her move from Arizona. ED vitals were BP 168/102 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, irregular, RR 24/min, Temp 98.0 F, SpO2 91% on room air improving to 96% on 2 L nasal cannula, weight 205 lb, and she reported orthopnea, 3-pillow sleep, abdominal bloating, and worsening bilateral leg edema. Admission studies showed BNP 1286 pg/mL, high-sensitivity troponin 19 ng/L then flat at 18 ng/L, creatinine 1.72 mg/dL rising transiently to 1.89 mg/dL during diuresis then improving to 1.63 mg/dL by discharge, eGFR nadir about 30 then 36, BUN 34 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mmol/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, glucose 238 mg/dL, hemoglobin A1c 8.8%, magnesium 1.8 mg/dL, and CBC without leukocytosis. Chest radiograph showed pulmonary vascular congestion with small bilateral pleural effusions. ECG demonstrated atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 122 bpm initially; telemetry later showed rate-controlled atrial fibrillation and intermittent sinus rhythm. Repeat transthoracic echocardiogram documented LVEF 33%, global LV hypokinesis, mildly reduced RV function, moderate left atrial enlargement, and no LV thrombus. She was treated with IV furosemide 40 mg twice daily, electrolyte repletion, restart of apixaban 5 mg twice daily, restart of carvedilol 6.25 mg twice daily once perfusion stabilized, re-initiation of sacubitril/valsartan 24/26 mg twice daily, continuation of empagliflozin 10 mg daily, insulin glargine 24 units nightly with correctional insulin, and atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. By discharge on 2024-04-25 her weight had fallen to 197 lb, dyspnea had improved, oxygen was no longer required, and discharge vitals were BP 128/78 mmHg and HR 82 bpm. The hospitalization generated ED documentation, admission H&P, daily progress notes, echocardiography report, discharge summary, medication reconciliation, heart-failure education materials, and instructions for daily weights, sodium restriction under 2 g/day, and heart-failure clinic follow-up within 1 week.

encounters.site_name: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic
encounters.reason: Post-discharge heart-failure follow-up and medication reconciliation
encounters.location: UNM Heart Failure Clinic, Albuquerque, New Mexico
encounters.header: 2024-05-03 — Office Visit — Post-discharge heart-failure follow-up and medication reconciliation
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker was seen at the UNM Heart Failure Clinic in Albuquerque, New Mexico by Marisol Chavez, NP, with medication review support from Kevin Romero, PharmD, 8 days after hospital discharge. Clinic vitals were BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 97.8 F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 194 lb, height 64 in, BMI 33.3 kg/m2. She reported much less orthopnea, improved exercise tolerance around the house, and no bleeding on apixaban, though she had mild dizziness when standing quickly. Medication reconciliation confirmed active long-term regimen of insulin glargine 24 units nightly, empagliflozin 10 mg daily, carvedilol 6.25 mg twice daily, sacubitril/valsartan 24/26 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg each morning, apixaban 5 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, and gabapentin 300 mg nightly; Romero documented manufacturer copay card assistance and transfer of prescriptions to a local Albuquerque pharmacy. Basic metabolic panel showed creatinine 1.58 mg/dL, eGFR about 38, BUN 31 mg/dL, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, and NT-proBNP 684 pg/mL, improved from hospitalization. HFrEF was assessed as improving to NYHA class II with euvolemic exam and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation remained controlled without current palpitations. Because blood pressure tolerated therapy, carvedilol was increased to 12.5 mg twice daily, while sacubitril/valsartan and furosemide doses were continued. A heart-failure clinic progress note, pharmacist medication access note, updated care plan, and patient instructions on weight logs and return precautions were completed, with labs planned again in 2 weeks and cardiology follow-up in 3 months.

encounters.reason: New nephrology consult for CKD stage 3b with albuminuria
encounters.location: Rio Grande Nephrology Associates, Albuquerque, New Mexico
encounters.header: 2024-06-17 — Office Visit — New nephrology consult for CKD stage 3b with albuminuria
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker established care at Rio Grande Nephrology Associates in Albuquerque, New Mexico with Dr. Helena Ortiz, MD, for evaluation of diabetic kidney disease after her recent heart-failure hospitalization. Vitals were BP 136/84 mmHg seated, HR 72 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 97.7 F, SpO2 98%, weight 193 lb, height 64 in, BMI 33.1 kg/m2. She brought home BP readings mostly in the 120s to 130s systolic and denied NSAID use, gross hematuria, or foamy urine changes beyond long-standing diabetes. Renal labs showed creatinine 1.61 mg/dL, eGFR 37 mL/min/1.73 m2, BUN 28 mg/dL, potassium 4.7 mmol/L, sodium 140 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, phosphorus 3.9 mg/dL, albumin 3.8 g/dL, intact PTH 74 pg/mL, 25-hydroxy vitamin D 28 ng/mL, hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, ferritin 118 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 24%, and urine albumin-creatinine ratio 286 mg/g with bland urine sediment. Dr. Ortiz assessed CKD stage 3b with persistent albuminuria due to diabetes and hypertension, with renal function also influenced by cardiorenal physiology and diuretic therapy. No biopsy or acute workup was indicated. Sacubitril/valsartan and empagliflozin were continued for renal and cardiac benefit, nephrotoxin avoidance was reinforced, and Denise was instructed not to use over-the-counter NSAIDs or contrast studies without notifying clinicians. A nephrology consultation note, CKD education handout, medication safety review, and plan for repeat BMP, urine albumin, and BP log review in about 4 months were completed.

encounters.reason: Diabetic retinopathy with macular edema evaluation and intravitreal treatment
encounters.location: Sandia Retina Specialists, Albuquerque, New Mexico
encounters.header: 2025-03-06 — Office Visit — Diabetic retinopathy with macular edema evaluation and intravitreal treatment
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker was evaluated at Sandia Retina Specialists in Albuquerque, New Mexico by Dr. Neha Kapur, MD, after referral for abnormal diabetic eye screening and blurred central vision in the left eye. Ophthalmic intake documented BP 126/78 mmHg, HR 71 bpm, weight 190 lb, and point-of-care glucose reported by patient log as 162 mg/dL that morning; no systemic office vitals beyond these were emphasized. Visual acuity was 20/25 OD and 20/50 OS with correction. Dilated fundus examination and OCT performed by Isaac Feldman, OD, showed moderate nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy in the right eye, severe nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy in the left eye, and center-involving diabetic macular edema in the left eye with central subfield thickness 412 microns; right macular thickness was 289 microns without center-involving edema. Intraocular pressures were 16 mmHg OD and 17 mmHg OS. Fundus photography documented scattered microaneurysms, dot-blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, and no retinal tear or detachment. Dr. Kapur diagnosed diabetic retinopathy with macular edema, discussed the relationship to longstanding hyperglycemia and blood-pressure control, and performed intravitreal bevacizumab 1.25 mg/0.05 mL injection in the left eye after topical anesthesia and povidone-iodine prep; Teresa Lucero, RN, provided procedure support and post-injection precautions. A retina consultation note, OCT report, fundus imaging report, procedure note, and post-injection instructions were generated, with follow-up planned in 4 to 6 weeks for repeat OCT and possible additional injection depending on response.

encounters.site_name: University of New Mexico Hospital and Heart Failure Clinic
encounters.reason: Chronic heart-failure and atrial fibrillation maintenance follow-up
encounters.location: UNM Heart Failure Clinic, Albuquerque, New Mexico
encounters.header: 2025-09-18 — Office Visit — Chronic heart-failure and atrial fibrillation maintenance follow-up
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker returned to the UNM Heart Failure Clinic in Albuquerque, New Mexico for longitudinal cardiology follow-up with Dr. Thomas Baca, MD. Vitals were BP 122/74 mmHg, HR 66 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 98.1 F, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 189 lb, height 64 in, BMI 32.4 kg/m2. She brought her home weight notebook and reported stable morning weights between 188 and 190 lb, mild exertional fatigue, and rare brief palpitations without syncope, chest pain, or recent edema. Physical examination showed no JVD, clear lungs, and only trace ankle edema. Labs reviewed from the same week showed creatinine 1.67 mg/dL, eGFR about 35, BUN 30 mg/dL, potassium 4.9 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, NT-proBNP 356 pg/mL, glucose 154 mg/dL, and hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm at 64 bpm with left ventricular hypertrophy and occasional premature atrial complexes. Chronic HFrEF was assessed as stable NYHA class II on guideline-directed therapy, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation remained controlled on anticoagulation without known thromboembolism. Medications were continued without change: carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, sacubitril/valsartan 24/26 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg each morning, empagliflozin 10 mg daily, apixaban 5 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, and insulin glargine managed with other clinicians. The cardiology progress note documented chronic management, review of renal limits on therapy escalation, and continued counseling on sodium restriction, daily weights, and when to call for weight gain over 3 lb in 24 hours; follow-up was planned in 6 months.

encounters.reason: CKD follow-up with potassium and blood-pressure review
encounters.location: Rio Grande Nephrology Associates, Albuquerque, New Mexico
encounters.header: 2026-02-11 — Office Visit — CKD follow-up with potassium and blood-pressure review
encounters.body_markdown: Denise Walker followed up at Rio Grande Nephrology Associates in Albuquerque, New Mexico with Omar Siddiqui, PA-C, for ongoing CKD management under Dr. Helena Ortiz's supervision. Intake vitals were BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 97.9 F, SpO2 98%, weight 187 lb, height 64 in, BMI 32.1 kg/m2. Home BP log reviewed by Rachel Montoya, RN, showed most readings between 118/70 and 132/78 mmHg, and Denise reported adherence to sodium restriction and avoidance of NSAIDs. Laboratory trend review showed creatinine 1.74 mg/dL, eGFR about 33, BUN 33 mg/dL, potassium 5.1 mmol/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L, phosphorus 4.1 mg/dL, calcium 9.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.9 g/dL, hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, urine albumin-creatinine ratio 248 mg/g, and glucose 148 mg/dL. CKD stage 3b with albuminuria was assessed as slowly progressive but outpatient-stable, with mild hyperkalemia risk related to ongoing guideline-directed heart-failure therapy. Because BP and albuminuria benefit still outweighed the potassium concern, sacubitril/valsartan was continued; she was counseled to moderate high-potassium foods, maintain hydration consistency, and repeat a BMP in 2 to 3 weeks. No dialysis planning was indicated. The encounter produced a nephrology follow-up note, reviewed lab summary, CKD diet counseling documentation, and a standing order for repeat renal panel and urine albumin testing before the next 6-month visit.
